picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO remains the highest-conviction AI infrastructure compounder in the universe: AI semiconductor revenue hit $10.8B in Q2 growing 143% YoY, Q3 guidance implies ~$16B AI revenue (>200% YoY), and record FCF of $10.3B (46% of revenue) funds the moat. The $30B+ multiyear Apple chip deal announced July 8 locks in a decade-long non-AI revenue floor while the AI ASIC franchise deepens with Anthropic and OpenAI added alongside Google. At ~$389 — 21% below its $495 ATH set June 3 — the stock is consolidating at the 200-day moving average after being sold off because management didn't raise AI guidance above already-staggering levels; 48 analysts maintain a Strong Buy with a $524 consensus target, implying 35% upside from current levels.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings report on September 3, 2026 — expected to confirm AI revenue run-rate approaching $16B+ with Q4 guide that exceeds current consensus
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$389, ~21% off June ATH of $495; consolidating at 200dma support; PEG of 0.63 on forward growth is compelling relative to 35%+ analyst upside target of $524; insider sales of $10M on July 8 are a minor flag but immaterial vs. institutional accumulation and Apple deal news
picks.exitTrigger: AI semiconductor revenue growth decelerates below 80% YoY in any quarter, or hyperscaler (Google, Meta, Apple) signals ASIC budget cuts; loss of a top-3 custom chip customer; FCF margin compresses below 35%
picks.keyRisk: Customer concentration — a small handful of large AI customers (Google primary) drives the bulk of AI revenue; any Google capex pullback would trigger violent repricing

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Berkshire Hathaway's most significant new bet under Greg Abel — a 225% stake increase to ~57.8M shares making it Berkshire's 5th largest holding — validates the thesis that Alphabet is the most attractively valued mega-cap AI compounder. Google Cloud grew +63% last quarter (accelerating from +48% in Q4 2025), the monetization question on Gemini has been 'satisfactorily answered,' and the $4.4T market cap company trades at a reasonable multiple relative to its FCF margin of ~28% and expanding ROIC. The soft June CPI (core at 2.6%) reduces the odds of a September hike, directly supporting multiple expansion for long-duration growth assets.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings report (expected late July 2026) — Google Cloud trajectory and Search/AI monetization update are the key readouts; Anthropic IPO in October (GOOGL is lead investor) is a secondary unlock
picks.catalystWindow: Next 2-3 weeks (Q2 earnings) and October 2026 (Anthropic IPO)
picks.entryNote: Market cap ~$4.4T; trading at a reasonable forward multiple vs. growth rate — 4/4 EPS beats with heavy institutional accumulation; Berkshire's $16.6B GOOGL position is the clearest smart-money buy signal in Q1 2026 13F filings; no extended valuation flag vs. peers
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY; Search market share loss to AI-native competitors exceeds 5 percentage points; DOJ antitrust outcome forces structural remedy beyond ad market
picks.keyRisk: Antitrust/regulatory overhang — ongoing DOJ search monopoly case could force structural remedies that impair Search's advertising moat

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman just reported the best quarter in its history on July 14: Q2 EPS of $20.98 (nearly doubled from $10.91 a year ago), net revenues of $20.34B (+39% YoY), ROE of 23.5%, and H1 2026 EPS of $38.51 vs. $25.07 in H1 2025. Structural tailwinds are durable — the SpaceX IPO generated ~$100M in underwriting fees alone, Goldman is co-lead on the Anthropic IPO targeting October 2026, and the steepening yield curve (10Y-2Y at +46bps) continues to widen net interest margins. At ~14x forward P/E vs. the broad market's 20x, Financials remain dramatically under-owned relative to tech.
picks.catalyst: Anthropic IPO (targeting October 2026) and ongoing AI-driven capital markets deal cycle; Q3 2026 earnings in mid-October confirming H2 investment banking acceleration
picks.catalystWindow: October 2026 (Anthropic IPO + Q3 earnings dual catalyst)
picks.entryNote: Financials sector ranked #1 in sector rotation scorecard; GS trading at ~14x forward P/E vs. 10-year median ~13.5x — fairly valued but earnings revisions are sharply positive; book value per share grew 2.8% in H1 2026 to $367.67; annualized ROE of 23.5% justifies a premium-to-book multiple
picks.exitTrigger: ROE falls below 15%; credit quality deterioration drives loan-loss provisions above $3B in a single quarter; investment banking revenue declines more than 30% on deal pipeline freeze
picks.keyRisk: A Fed-induced credit cycle turn — if the hawkish hike scenario materializes and corporate deal activity freezes, capital markets revenues collapse disproportionately

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta delivered Q1 2026 revenue of $56.3B (+33% YoY), operating income of $22.9B at a 41% margin, and EPS of $10.44 — exceeding analyst expectations despite a 7% post-earnings dip that now represents a superior entry point. The Meta Compute cloud infrastructure launch monetizes $40B+ in annual AI capex as a revenue stream rather than a pure cost, creating a new growth vector that transforms Meta from a pure ad-tech compounder into an AI infrastructure provider. Tepper (largest position rotation into AMZN but maintained meta exposure) and institutional dip-buyers are active; ad monetization of AI-native features (Llama-integrated Reels, AI personas) has not yet been fully priced.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July) — first full quarter reflecting Meta Compute revenue recognition and Llama-integrated ad product monetization
picks.entryNote: Post-Q1 dip improved risk/reward; 41% operating margin provides downside protection even in a revenue growth slowdown; ~$50B+ annual FCF; 4/4 EPS beats; sector rotation scorecard ranks Communication Services #4 with Meta Compute as the key structural upgrade catalyst
picks.exitTrigger: Ad revenue growth decelerates below 15% YoY; Meta Compute fails to reach $5B revenue run-rate by end of 2026; reality labs losses exceed $20B annually with no monetization path
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory fragmentation — EU digital markets act enforcement or US antitrust action targeting WhatsApp/Instagram integration could force structural separation of ad revenue streams

picks.name: Micron Technology Inc.
picks.rationale: Micron's Q3 2026 EPS of $25.11 beat the $20.20 consensus by 24% — an extraordinary upside surprise — with Q4 guidance of $49-51B revenue (vs. $43.5B consensus) and EPS guidance of $30-32 (vs. $25.43 consensus), implying 86% gross margins driven by HBM3E pricing power in the AI data center stack. With 8 consecutive EPS beats averaging 15.8% upside, the Bernstein downgrade to Underperform at $750 target (citing mean reversion concerns) is a known bear case the market has partially absorbed, as HBM supply remains structurally constrained through 2027 — the memory chip shortage is expected to last several more years. The stock has pulled back from highs, offering a re-entry as HBM ramp accelerates.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings (September 2026) — confirmation that $50B quarterly revenue and ~86% gross margins are sustainable into FY2027; HBM3E share gains vs. SK Hynix
picks.entryNote: 52-week performance ~+700%; Bernstein downgrade at $750 target represents a credible contrarian bear case but HBM structural scarcity is the bull's answer; trading at 12.2x forward earnings vs. 8.5x historical average — premium justified by the secular HBM demand shift; highest-momentum name on the screen but most volatile; size modestly
picks.exitTrigger: HBM average selling price declines more than 20% QoQ suggesting oversupply; hyperscaler AI capex cuts reduce HBM order book by more than 30%; SK Hynix achieves dominant HBM supply share above 70%
picks.keyRisk: Traditional DRAM cycle mean reversion — if AI capex slows materially, Micron's commodity DRAM business reverts to prior-cycle dynamics with violent margin compression, as Bernstein warns

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is the highest-quality compounder in the portfolio — a ratings/data/analytics oligopoly with ~60% incremental margins and structural tailwinds from rising global debt issuance (higher rates = more frequent refinancing = more rating fees), AI-driven data monetization, and the active capital markets cycle benefiting Platts Commodity Intelligence and Market Intelligence divisions. In a stagflationary late-cycle regime, SPGI's pricing power (fees indexed to issuance volume, not GDP growth) and asset-light model generate durable earnings growth uncorrelated to the AI capex cycle. It has appeared in every prior positioning day this week, validating thesis continuity.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July) — watch for debt issuance volume commentary and Market Intelligence subscription growth rate; Anthropic and SpaceX IPO rating mandates are incremental fee events
picks.catalystWindow: Next 2-3 weeks (Q2 earnings); ongoing capital markets activity through Q4 2026
picks.entryNote: Trading at roughly 28-30x forward earnings — a premium justified by 60%+ incremental margins and ~35% FCF yield on equity; Gartner (a comparable quality-data compounder) is up 6.1% in July as smart money validates the quality-data thesis; SPGI has been in the top-10 growth book continuously since July 3
picks.exitTrigger: Debt issuance volume collapses more than 40% YoY (i.e., full credit freeze); AI-native data competitors erode Market Intelligence subscription renewal rates below 90%; regulatory breakup of the Ratings/Intelligence combined entity
picks.keyRisk: Credit market seizure — a Fed-forced hike cycle that kills corporate bond issuance would directly impair Ratings revenue, which is the highest-margin segment

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier geopolitical hedge in the growth book — a defense compounder with a $220B+ backlog exposed to every structural tailwind in today's macro: Iran conflict driving US and NATO defense budgets, NATO 2%+ GDP commitment by member states, and next-generation missile defense (Patriot PAC-3, StormBreaker) demand surging. The combination of a long-cycle government order book and a commercial aerospace MRO (Maintenance, Repair and Overhaul) recovery at Pratt & Whitney makes RTX a genuine dual-engine grower insulated from consumer cycles. Has been in the top-10 growth book every day since July 3.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July 2026) — defense backlog update and Pratt & Whitney GTF engine remediation cost clarity; any NATO spending increase announcement or new US arms package to Middle East allies
picks.catalystWindow: Next 2-3 weeks (Q2 earnings) and ongoing throughout 2026-2027 as Iran conflict drives defense authorization increases
picks.entryNote: Industrials sector ranked #3 in rotation scorecard; RTX at ~19x forward P/E, consistent with the sector median, but backlog quality justifies premium; defense spending is a secular budget item, not a cyclical discretionary — provides genuine counter-cyclical revenue stability
picks.exitTrigger: Comprehensive Iran peace deal removes geopolitical premium and triggers NATO budget rollbacks; Pratt & Whitney GTF engine recall costs exceed $10B total provision; loss of major US Air Force contract rebid
picks.keyRisk: GTF engine remediation — Pratt & Whitney's geared turbofan blade inspection program could face unanticipated cost overruns or customer compensation demands that impair margins for 2-3 more years

picks.name: Vistra Energy Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the purest expression of the AI power demand thesis in the utility sector — Tepper doubled his Vistra position in Q1 2026 as the most direct bet on data center electricity demand. With hyperscalers spending $754B in AI capex in 2026, power is the bottleneck, and Vistra's diversified generation mix (nuclear baseload + gas peaking + retail) positions it as the merchant power compounder best placed to capture premium contracted and spot prices. The HALO trade (Heavy Assets, Low Obsolescence) identified in the sector rotation update as a multi-month structural theme directly benefits VST. Nuclear baseload assets (Comanche Peak, Zimmer) are irreplaceable long-duration assets with AI data center co-location appeal.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected early August) — power price realization, data center contracted volume announcements, and nuclear co-location deal pipeline update
picks.entryNote: Has been in the growth book since July 7; Tepper at Appaloosa doubled the position in Q1 2026, the clearest institutional validator; Utilities sector is rate-sensitive but VST's merchant power model and nuclear assets make it structurally different from regulated utility yield plays; soft CPI today reduces rate headwind modestly
picks.exitTrigger: Power purchase agreement (PPA) prices for data centers collapse due to demand overshoot or grid buildout acceleration; nuclear plant operational issues requiring extended outages; FERC capacity market rule changes that compress merchant margins
picks.keyRisk: Power price mean reversion — if AI capex build slows or grid supply additions (new gas, solar) outpace demand growth faster than expected, merchant power prices could normalize sharply downward

picks.name: Quanta Services Inc.
picks.rationale: PWR is the critical-infrastructure picks-and-shovels play on the AI power buildout — the dominant electrical contractor for grid transmission, substation construction, and data center power infrastructure. With $754B in hyperscaler AI capex driving unprecedented power demand, Quanta's backlog of multi-year electrical construction contracts is structurally growing regardless of which AI chip or cloud company wins. PWR cannot be disrupted by AI software efficiency gains — grid construction is a decades-long physical buildout that requires PWR's specialized labor force. Has been in the top-10 growth book every day since July 3, validating persistent institutional demand.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July/early August) — backlog update and utility/data center segment margin trajectory; any major grid infrastructure legislation or FERC interconnection queue reform accelerates the thesis
picks.catalystWindow: Next 3-5 weeks (Q2 earnings) and ongoing 2026-2028 as grid investment cycle accelerates
picks.entryNote: Industrials sector ranked #3 with confirmed leadership RS; PWR is differentiated from defense peers by its direct tie to AI data center construction spending — a more secular growth driver than pure cyclical industrial names; ~19x forward P/E is fair for 15-20% EPS CAGR backed by contracted multi-year backlog
picks.exitTrigger: Backlog growth decelerates below 5% YoY; labor cost inflation or craft workforce shortages compress operating margins below 8%; AI capex cycle peaks and utility transmission investment programs are deferred by regulatory delay
picks.keyRisk: Labor and materials cost inflation — Quanta's fixed-price contracts expose margins to cost overruns if wages for specialized electrical workers rise faster than contract escalators

picks.name: CrowdStrike Holdings Inc.
picks.rationale: Cybersecurity is the non-AI-capex-dependent secular growth driver in Technology — CRWD's AI-native Falcon platform benefits from AI proliferation (every new AI workload expands the attack surface requiring endpoint and cloud security coverage) without being exposed to hyperscaler capex budget cuts. With geopolitical risk elevated (Iran conflict, NATO tensions), state-sponsored cyber threats against critical infrastructure and financial systems are rising sharply, expanding CRWD's government and financial sector pipeline. CRWD appeared in the growth book from July 7 through July 13, reflecting consistent institutional momentum.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings (expected August 2026) — net new ARR acceleration and platform consolidation wins (Charlotte AI agent adoption rate) are the key metrics; any major cyber incident or government mandate driving platform consolidation to Falcon
picks.catalystWindow: August 2026 (Q2 FY2027 earnings); ongoing geopolitical threat backdrop creates a continuous pull-forward of enterprise security spending
picks.entryNote: Cybersecurity sector confirmed as a preferred sub-sector within Technology despite XLK's overall weakening RS signal — CRWD is the platform leader in AI-native security with 20%+ ARR growth and expanding gross margins; valuation is high (~60-65x forward earnings) but platform stickiness (130%+ NRR) justifies a premium growth multiple
picks.exitTrigger: Net new ARR growth decelerates below 15%; a second major operational incident (following the July 2024 outage) triggers customer contract terminations above 5% of ARR; Microsoft Defender platform consolidation wins begin materially eroding CRWD enterprise seat count
picks.keyRisk: Elevated valuation leaves no room for execution error — any ARR miss or guidance cut at these multiples would result in a 25-35% repricing, as seen with comparable high-multiple SaaS names

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is the highest-conviction macro hedge in the late-cycle stagflationary regime: IAU/GLD is up ~+129.5% since early 2024, geopolitical risk from the Iran conflict (US reinstating naval blockade, oil spiking) is a structural positive for safe-haven demand, and the soft June CPI today (while reducing near-term hike urgency) doesn't eliminate the risk that oil re-acceleration forces inflation back above 4% within 2-3 months. Gold has zero correlation to AI capex cycle risk and strong negative correlation to real rates — the single tail risk scenario where BOTH AI and the macro turn simultaneously is the scenario where GLD performs best. Central bank buying from EM reserve diversification provides a structurally supportive bid beneath spot.
picks.catalyst: Any re-escalation of Iran conflict that drives oil back above $90; Core PCE print on July 30 coming in above 3.0% would reignite hike fears and safe-haven demand; Fed dovish pivot confirmation in 2027 would be the multi-year breakout catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing — July 30 PCE print is nearest catalyst; Iran conflict is a live daily risk
picks.entryNote: Up ~9.8% YTD per risk briefing; +129.5% since early 2024; consolidating at recent highs — ideal defensive position size as an insurance layer; tactical entry on any pullback to the $265-270 range per risk officer briefing
picks.exitTrigger: Comprehensive Iran peace deal combined with core PCE falling sustainably below 2.5% removes both geopolitical and inflation premium; Fed initiates clear easing cycle with real rate declining below -1%
picks.keyRisk: A rapid and comprehensive Iran peace deal combined with a dramatic energy price collapse would remove the geopolitical and inflation premium simultaneously, compressing gold 10-15% from current levels

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides the portfolio's core income and quality factor ballast — a 3.20% dividend yield, 0.06% expense ratio, and systematic exclusion of money-losing companies tilts exposure toward cash-flow compounders (Financials, Healthcare, Consumer Staples) that outperform in late-cycle environments. SCHD's value/quality tilt gives it natural hedging properties against the AI-bubble unwind tail risk, as it has minimal overlap with high-multiple growth names that dominate the S&P 500. The equal-weighted S&P 500 (RSP) outperforming cap-weighted in early 2026 validates the quality-rotation thesis that SCHD structurally captures.
picks.catalyst: Defensive rotation acceleration if Q3 earnings season shows broad earnings misses outside AI/Financials; yield spread compression as inflation cools would make SCHD's 3.2% yield more competitive vs. risk-free rates
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing through 2026-2027 earnings cycle; benefits continuously from dividend reinvestment compounding
picks.entryNote: 30B AUM, 0.06% expense ratio, 3.20% dividend yield; low correlation to Nasdaq provides genuine diversification benefit; has been in the defensive sleeve every trading day since July 3 — maximum conviction carry-over
picks.exitTrigger: Dividend cuts from core holdings exceeding 20% of portfolio weight simultaneously; credit crisis that impairs Financials holdings (SCHD's largest sector) disproportionately; yield advantage over money markets disappears if Fed cuts rates aggressively
picks.keyRisk: Value trap risk if the stagflationary regime persists and Financials (SCHD's largest weight) face credit cycle deterioration from the rate-hike scenario — SCHD is defensive but not immune to a credit event

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire under Greg Abel is the ultimate all-weather defensive holding: $397B in cash/T-bills (earning risk-free 3.63% while waiting for distressed opportunities), diversified operating earnings from GEICO, BNSF, and Berkshire Hathaway Energy, plus the newly-concentrated equity portfolio with Alphabet as the #5 holding. Beta ~0.85 provides meaningful downside cushion. In a late-cycle regime where the index is priced at 20x forward earnings with no valuation cushion, Berkshire's 1.4x book is historically reasonable — and Abel's disciplined posture (cash still grew from $373B to $397B in Q1) signals that distressed-entry optionality is preserved for the credit event scenario that tail risk #3 models.
picks.catalyst: Berkshire Q2 2026 earnings (early August) — operating earnings trajectory and any announcement of a major acquisition or stock buyback acceleration; a broad market correction that triggers Abel's first major capital deployment decision
picks.catalystWindow: Early August 2026 (Q2 earnings); ongoing optionality through any 2026-2027 credit event
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~1.4x book, historically reasonable; $397B cash earns ~$14B annually at current short rates — pure optionality value; has been in the defensive sleeve every day since July 3; the GOOGL position (5th largest holding) gives indirect AI exposure without direct AI-capex-cycle risk
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel makes a major acquisition at a materially overpaid multiple (destroying capital discipline); operating businesses (GEICO, BNSF) deteriorate simultaneously; stock re-rates above 2x book without acquisition catalyst
picks.keyRisk: Opportunity cost risk — if the bull case for AI accelerates further without a correction, Berkshire's cash pile is a structural drag on relative performance vs. the growth book

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides the portfolio's duration hedge at a pivotal moment — today's soft June CPI (headline -0.4% MoM, core flat, annual headline 3.5% vs. 3.8% consensus) caused Treasury yields to fall sharply, and the 7-10Y belly of the curve is the most sensitive to disinflation repricing. With the 10Y at 4.62% (per today's risk briefing intraday) and a 51% September hike probability that just dropped from 75%+ pre-CPI, IEF offers a position that profits if the disinflation narrative takes hold while capping downside to the still-hawkish Fed scenarios. Medium conviction reflects the genuine two-sided risk — the Iran oil wildcard could reverse today's progress and drive yields higher again.
picks.catalyst: Core PCE print on July 30, 2026 — if it follows CPI lower (which it likely will given today's flat core CPI), September hike odds drop further and the 10Y rally extends; FOMC July 28-29 statement removing explicit hike language would be an additional rally catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: July 28-30, 2026 (FOMC decision + PCE print in a 48-hour compressed window)
picks.entryNote: 10Y UST at 4.62% intraday today — above the macro briefing's 4.33% baseline, suggesting the Iran/geopolitical risk premium is elevated and any de-escalation would provide additional bond tailwind; yields fell sharply post-CPI print; moderate position size (5%) appropriate given two-sided risk
picks.exitTrigger: 10Y Treasury yield breaks above 5.0% on a sustained basis (indicating Fed hike expectations are repriced fully higher); core PCE on July 30 comes in above 3.2% reversing today's disinflation signal
picks.keyRisk: Iran oil re-escalation drives energy back above $90/barrel and reverses the June CPI disinflation signal, forcing the Fed to hike in September and driving 10Y yields back toward 4.8-5.0%

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: Coca-Cola anchors the portfolio's Consumer Staples defensive exposure: 64 consecutive years of dividend increases, ~60% international revenue providing an implicit USD hedge against the DXY's current 101-103 range, and a beta of ~0.55 that acts as a genuine shock absorber when VIX spikes. In the late-cycle regime, KO's pricing power (demonstrated through tariff and inflation pass-through since 2022) and truly non-discretionary demand profile mean revenue is largely immune to the growth slowdown the Atlanta Fed GDPNow is signaling at 1.3% SAAR. Has been in the defensive sleeve continuously since July 3.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July) — organic revenue growth rate and volume/price mix commentary in emerging markets; USD weakening on CPI surprise provides a sequential international revenue tailwind
picks.entryNote: Trading at 22-23x forward earnings — not cheap but reasonable for the moat duration and 64-year dividend growth streak; beta of ~0.55 makes it one of the lowest-correlation names to tech/AI risk in the universe; has been in every defensive sleeve since July 3
picks.exitTrigger: Organic revenue growth decelerates below 3% YoY for two consecutive quarters; significant market share loss in emerging markets to local competitors; USD strengthens sharply above DXY 110 impairing international earnings translation
picks.keyRisk: Volume pressure from GLP-1 weight-loss drug adoption reducing caloric beverage consumption — a multi-year secular headwind if Lilly/Novo's obesity drugs achieve 20%+ penetration in core demographics

summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle stagflationary regime with AI infrastructure as the dominant structural tailwind, operating against a backdrop where June CPI surprised meaningfully to the downside (headline 3.5%, core 2.6%) but the Iran conflict and Fed hawkishness (Warsh: 'not mission accomplished') keep the macro outcome genuinely two-sided. The 10-name growth book concentrates in the three highest-conviction secular themes — AI infrastructure compounders (AVGO, MU), AI platform monetizers (GOOGL, META), power/grid beneficiaries of the AI capex supercycle (VST, PWR), and the Financials earnings breakout that just delivered (GS), supplemented by quality compounder SPGI, defense geopolitical hedge RTX, and cybersecurity secular grower CRWD as differentiated non-AI-capex exposures. The 5-name defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) is structured to perform in three distinct tail scenarios: GLD and IEF hedge the inflation-vs-disinflation binary, SCHD and KO provide quality income ballast against a growth slowdown, and BRK.B's $397B cash fortress provides optionality for the credit event scenario. Carry-overs from the prior week's book include AVGO (#1 throughout), GOOGL, META, MU, SPGI, RTX, PWR, VST as confirmed positions — GS is elevated from #6/8 after today's historic Q2 print (EPS $20.98, nearly doubled YoY), while CRWD returns replacing TSM, which is trimmed from the book given Druckenmiller's 9% Q1 reduction and the XLK weakening RS signal. The dominant risk being underwritten is AI capex cycle peak risk — if any hyperscaler signals material capex reduction, AVGO, MU, VST, and PWR would all reprice simultaneously, which is why the 5-name defensive sleeve carries 29% of the portfolio weight.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans 5 distinct GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, MU, CRWD — 3 names, the maximum allowed), Communication Services (GOOGL, META — 2 names at different points in the AI value chain: infrastructure vs. platform/ad monetization), Financials (GS, SPGI — capital markets vs. data/ratings), Industrials (RTX, PWR — defense vs. grid construction), and Utilities (VST — merchant power/nuclear); AVGO and MU share AI semiconductor exposure but occupy distinct positions (custom ASIC/networking vs. HBM memory), while RTX and PWR share Industrials classification but are economically uncorrelated (defense procurement vs. grid construction contracting). The defensive sleeve spans 5 distinct exposures with no duplication: gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality equity (SCHD), conglomerate/cash optionality (BRK.B), intermediate-duration Treasuries (IEF), and consumer staples/income (KO).

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.

The just-announced $30B Apple chip deal extending through 2031 adds a new contracted revenue anchor and triggered a 5% single-day rally on July 6, demonstrating institutional demand. With AI semiconductor revenue up 143% YoY and FY2026 consensus revenue at ~$106B (+40%), estimate revisions remain firmly upward, and the next earnings catalyst on September 3 gives a 7-week runway to accumulate into any weakness.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings on September 3, 2026 — with guidance reset on AI XPU revenue following Apple deal
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$400, down ~19% from its June 3 ATH of $495, consolidating above the 200-day MA. P/E ~67x TTM but ~25x on FY2027 estimates; Erste Group downgraded on valuation but Cantor Fitzgerald maintains $525 target. Entry here captures the pullback with next catalyst 7 weeks out.
picks.exitTrigger: Any single hyperscaler signals capex pause or displaces AVGO XPU with proprietary silicon at scale; AI semiconductor revenue growth decelerates below 80% YoY in next print
picks.keyRisk: Customer concentration — a handful of large AI customers (Google, Apple, Anthropic) drive the bulk of incremental revenue; loss of one is a 20%+ thesis break

picks.name: Micron Technology Inc.
picks.rationale: Micron's Q3 FY2026 results were extraordinary: record $41.5B revenue, 84.9% gross margin (company record), and a Q4 revenue guide of $50B — 16% above the $42.9B analyst consensus. HBM3E and HBM4 products are fully booked through 2027 with $22B in strategic agreements secured including $18B in cash deposits, making near-term demand essentially locked in. The sector rotation scorecard flags MU as the #1 ranked name with a 97/100 factor score; it has beaten EPS estimates 8 consecutive quarters with a 15.8% average surprise. Druckenmiller's pivot toward memory/storage names (SanDisk, MU-adjacent) corroborates the AI memory supercycle thesis.

picks.exitTrigger: HBM bookings for 2028 fail to materialize or a major customer (Samsung, SK Hynix) announces HBM capacity expansion that threatens pricing discipline above $22B contracted base

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta delivered Q1 2026 revenue of $56.31B (+33.1% YoY) with operational EPS of ~$7.31 (stripping the one-time tax benefit), demonstrating durable advertising pricing power — average price per ad rose 12%. With a 2-year forward revenue CAGR of ~22.5%, strong FCF generation ($12.39B in Q1), and AI monetization across 3B+ daily active users, META offers rare growth-quality combination in a rate-sensitive environment. Ackman's Pershing Square holds META at 11%+ of portfolio; it remains in the Communication Services sector yet functions as a tech-quality compounder and diversifies away from pure semiconductor exposure.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July 2026) — key read on ad pricing resilience and Llama AI monetization update, plus any update on custom AI chip production starting September 2026
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$578; at approximately 22x fwd P/E on FY2027 estimates, trading at a modest discount to its 3-year average given elevated CapEx ($125–145B guide for 2026). The sell-off post-CapEx raise created a technical consolidation base; Q2 earnings are the re-rating event.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company

In the current stagflationary regime, KO benefits from its ability to pass through commodity and input cost inflation via pricing (demonstrated in 2021–2024) while its 3%+ dividend yield provides income. Berkshire Hathaway's 11.56% portfolio weight in KO is the single most durable smart money validation in the universe. weeklyReport.summary: This is a late-cycle stagflationary regime: GDPNow at 1.3%, payrolls at +57K, CPI at 4.2%, and a Fed debating hikes rather than cuts — the classic environment where earnings leadership is narrow, real growth is softening, and inflation prevents policy rescue. The growth book expresses this regime through three lenses: (1) AI infrastructure compounders with locked-in demand (AVGO, MU, CRWD) that are insulated from the consumption-driven slowdown; (2) geopolitical/inflation beneficiaries (VST, PWR, RTX) where the Iran conflict and energy-demand supercycle create multi-year earnings revision tailwinds; and (3) high-quality franchises (META, GOOGL, SPGI, LLY) with pricing power and secular growth immune to the credit cycle. Core carry-overs from prior days — AVGO (#1), MU (#2), META (#3), GOOGL (#4), SPGI (#5), RTX (#6), VST (#7), CRWD (#8), PWR (#9) — all maintain intact theses with no deterioration; LLY replaces ISRG/GS at rank 10 as the single best risk-adjusted healthcare compounder at a discounted sector entry. The defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) hedges the three dominant tail risks: fiscal/bond market vigilante return (GLD + IEF), AI capex cycle reversal (SCHD + BRK.B), and stagflation re-ignition (KO + GLD). The dominant risk being underwritten is that AI capex revision momentum stalls following Q2 earnings — a scenario where AVGO, MU, and META would all face simultaneous multiple compression.
weeklyReport.diversificationNote: Growth book spans 5 distinct GICS sectors — Information Technology (AVGO, MU, CRWD — the maximum 3), Communication Services (META, GOOGL), Industrials (RTX, PWR), Utilities (VST), Financials (SPGI), and Health Care (LLY); within IT, AVGO (custom silicon/networking), MU (memory/HBM), and CRWD (cybersecurity) represent three distinct points in the AI value chain. The defensive sleeve spans gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality income (SCHD), diversified financial conglomerate (BRK.B), long-duration Treasuries (IEF), and consumer staples (KO) — five distinct exposures with no duplication, providing coverage across the inflation-hedge, income, equity-quality, duration, and recession-defense dimensions.

phaseData.macro:
Here is your senior macro strategy briefing as of **Friday, July 10, 2026**.

---

## 📊 MACRO DATA DASHBOARD — US EQUITIES REGIME BRIEF

**Fed Policy**

The Fed funds target range sits at **3.50%–3.75%**, unchanged since the Fed cut by 75bps total in late 2024–2025.
 
The June dot plot (released June 17) shows the median end-2026 dot has been lifted to **3.8%**, implying at least one hike is on the table.
 
Rate cuts are essentially off the table for the rest of the year; nine policymakers project at least one hike, with six suggesting multiple hikes.
 
Markets are pricing in at least one Fed rate hike by end-2026, with the probability of a September move currently around **64%**.
 
The longer-run neutral anchor is heavily concentrated at **3.0%**, meaning the real policy rate (~+0.6% above neutral on headline PCE) is modestly restrictive but far less so than the 2022–2023 peak.


**Yield Curve**

The **10-year Treasury** eased to ~**4.54%** on July 10.
 
The **30-year** is at **5.05%** and the **2-year** at **4.19%**.
 
The **2s10s spread is +36 bps** and the **10Y-3M gap is +69 bps**, both comfortably positive.
 The curve has re-steepened from its deep 2022–2024 inversion but remains narrow by historical expansion standards. 
Market-based inflation expectations have firmed, with the **5-year breakeven at 2.28%** and the **10-year at 2.25%**.


**Inflation**

Headline CPI hit **4.2% YoY** in May 2026 — the highest since April 2023 — driven by energy costs surging **23.5%** due to the Iran conflict shock.
 
Core CPI rose to **2.9% YoY**, a new post-2025 high, though the monthly core gain was only **+0.2%**, below the prior month's 0.4%.
 
Headline PCE printed at **4.07% YoY** in May, running at a **6.28% annualized pace** over the past three months.
 
Core PCE was **3.3%** as of April (latest), indicating underlying inflation pressures remain elevated.
 The trajectory is **re-accelerating** on headline (energy-driven) but with core showing some stickiness rather than clean deceleration.

**Growth**

The Atlanta Fed **GDPNow for Q2 2026 stands at 1.3%** (SAAR) as of July 8, down sharply from 3.1% in late June, with inventory drag weighing.
 
Q1 2026 real GDP grew at **2.1% annualized** per the BEA third estimate.
 
**ISM Manufacturing PMI** came in at **53.3** in June — its sixth consecutive month above 50, ending a prior 10-month contraction.
 
The June employment report showed **payrolls rising only +57,000**, roughly half of consensus expectations, a meaningful soft patch.
 
Unemployment holds at **4.3%**, unchanged over the past year.


**Credit & Liquidity**

HY OAS (ICE BofA) is in the **280–330 bps range**, consistent with 2024–2026 tight historical levels.
 
BBB IG spreads were near **~100 bps in April 2026, near 25-year tights**.
 Credit is NOT signaling stress — spreads are in late-cycle complacency territory. 
The DXY regained the **~101 level** as inflation and tariff uncertainty kept rate-cut expectations muted.


**Earnings**

As of July 8, the S&P 500 is at **7,482.71**. Forward 12-month EPS estimate is **$374.46/share**, yielding a **forward P/E of ~20x** vs. a trailing P/E of **24.7x**.
 
The forward P/E of **20.4x** is above both the 5-year average of **19.9x** and the 10-year average of **19.0x**.
 
Crucially, Q2 estimates were revised **UP** by 3.4% during the quarter — the opposite of the historical norm.
 
Energy (+61.5%) and Information Technology (+8.7%) are leading estimate revisions, with IT also having the highest positive EPS guidance count.
 
Full-year 2026 earnings growth is forecast at **+24.1% YoY**.


**Geopolitical / Event Tape**

Escalating US-Iran hostilities have driven a sharp energy price spike, stoking fears of another inflation bout and reinforcing a higher-for-longer Fed posture.
 
A US Court of International Trade ruled the administration exceeded its authority on the Section 122 tariff surcharge; the ruling adds legal uncertainty to trade policy while appeal proceeds.


---

## ¶1 — REGIME CALL

**This is a late-cycle regime with stagflationary overtones.** 
The FOMC itself characterized the economy as "expanding at a solid pace" while acknowledging "inflation remains elevated relative to the 2% goal, in part reflecting supply shocks."
 The combination of a re-accelerating headline CPI (4.2%), a GDPNow that has collapsed to 1.3% from 3.1% in just two weeks, a payroll print of only +57K, and a Fed that has removed its easing bias and is now debating hikes is textbook late-cycle: nominal growth is decelerating, real growth is softening, yet inflation is NOT cooperating with policy, compressing the Fed's ability to ease. 
The curve shape — front end anchored, belly flat, long end modestly higher — is "a classic late-cycle signature."


---

## ¶2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** 
Energy and Information Technology are the clear earnings leaders this cycle, with Energy EPS estimates up 61.5% and IT driving the bulk of positive guidance revisions.
 Energy benefits directly from the geopolitical oil shock (Iran war premium, Strait of Hormuz disruption). 
AI infrastructure spending is the catalyst driving roughly half of S&P 500 earnings growth, concentrated in a cluster of mega-cap names.
 
Tech shows positive earnings sentiment and strong price momentum, while Energy keeps valuations constructive alongside strong fundamental support.
 Nominal-sensitive value sectors (Financials, select Industrials) also benefit from a steeper back-end curve (+69 bps 10Y-3M). Credit markets are no obstacle — 
below 350 bps HY OAS signals late-cycle complacency, not systemic risk.


**Breaking:** Rate-sensitive, long-duration and consumer-facing names are under pressure. The +57K payroll print and 1.3% GDPNow signal consumer spending fatigue. 
A hot CPI print, higher Treasury yields, or weaker guidance can compress multiples easily, especially since the S&P 500 price rose 14.6% since March while forward EPS rose only 10.8% — price has outrun earnings.
 
Trailing P/E at ~25x and Shiller CAPE at 39x signal the market is historically expensive on realized earnings.
 Small caps and highly leveraged issuers face a dual squeeze from tight credit standards and a potential hike cycle resumption. 
Concentration risk is acute — mega-cap AI names generated the majority of index gains, making the index increasingly a single-factor bet.


---

## ¶3 — WHAT TO WATCH (Next 1–6 Months)


**Three highest-impact catalysts: July 14 CPI, July 28–29 FOMC meeting, and July 30 PCE report.**


1. 🔥 **July 14 CPI (June print)** — 
With the forward P/E at 20.4x vs. a 10-year average of 19.0x, a hot print is the single most immediate trigger that could break the market's July winning streak and reprice the September hike probability sharply higher.


2. ⚖️ **July 28–29 FOMC (Warsh's second meeting)** — 
Policymakers are expected to hold rates but preserve the option to tighten further if inflation proves persistent.
 
The committee has already erased easing language and pushed any cuts into 2027–2028 as they weigh the durability of inflation from the Iran war energy shock.
 Any explicit hike signal or formal tightening bias language would be the most significant policy repricing event of H2 2026.

3. 🌍 **US-Iran ceasefire / escalation resolution** — 
The 10-year briefly eased as reports emerged that the US and Iran would continue peace negotiations; any durable de-escalation would immediately collapse the energy premium in CPI, potentially reopening the cuts narrative and providing a significant risk-asset tailwind.
 Conversely, a wider regional conflict closing the Strait of Hormuz would push Brent materially higher and fully validate the hawkish dot plot.

---
*All data as of July 10, 2026. Sources: FOMC, BLS, BEA, Atlanta Fed GDPNow, FactSet Earnings Insight, Convex/ICE BofA, primerates.com, TradingEconomics, StreetStats.*

# 🏦 SECTOR ROTATION SCORECARD — July 10, 2026

Economic growth is rebounding but consumers are strained by negative real wage growth and rising energy costs. Earnings are driving the bull market, but leadership is narrow and concentrated in AI and energy-related sectors.
 
AI infrastructure stocks have seen 2026 earnings estimates revised higher by more than 50% since December 2024, while the S&P 500 excluding AI infrastructure has seen estimates move down slightly.
 
The equal-weighted S&P 500 has outperformed the cap-weighted index in 2026 — one of the clearest indications of a broader rotation that investors haven't seen in years.


---

## 🥇 TIER 1 — OVERWEIGHT (High Conviction Longs)

---

### 1. 🖥️ Technology / AI Infrastructure / Semis (XLK, SMH, SOXX)
**Relative Strength Tier: LEADER**


XLK leads all sector SPDRs with a **+33% YTD gain** in H1 2026, despite a June pullback fueled by investor apprehension over AI infrastructure spending and the Fed hinting toward future rate hikes.


- **Valuation/Flow:** 
In Q2 2026 alone, over **$12 billion** flowed into technology ETFs.
 
XLK performance is supported by heavy portfolio weighting toward semiconductor developers, who have seen strong gains throughout 2026.
 
Nvidia (14.66% weight) returned +5.4% YTD; **Micron (5.52% weight) surged over 290%**, driven by its storage and memory chip business.
 Valuation is **rich** vs. 10-year median; fwd P/E ~28–30x vs. historical ~21x.
- **Earnings Revisions:** 
In Technology, the outsized EPS revision is concentrated among Sandisk, Micron, Intel, and Broadcom.
 Breadth is narrow — index-level revisions are driven by a handful of semi names.
- **Business Cycle Role:** Late expansion/structural secular — AI capex is a multi-year supercycle.
- **Why Now:** 
Goldman Sachs estimates the largest hyperscalers will spend nearly **$800 billion on capex this year** (+80% YoY), with another $900B+ projected in 2027 — capex-to-cash-flow ratios reminiscent of the late 1990s.
 The semi sub-cycle (HBM memory, custom ASICs) has years of runway; own it on dips, but size tactically given concentration risk. 
The key risk: large AI capex may not translate to the strong future earnings that investors currently expect.


---

### 2. 🏭 Industrials — Defense / Electrification / Aerospace (XLI, ITA, ICLN)
**Relative Strength Tier: LEADER**


XLI rose **+20% in H1 2026**, fueled by the sustained AI data center buildout, steady demand for aerospace and defense projects, and increased commodity equipment upgrades.


- **Valuation/Flow:** 
XLI delivered a **total return of +25.19% over the past year**, including dividends.
 Valuation is **fair-to-slightly-rich** (~21x fwd P/E vs. ~18x 10-year median) but supported by revision upgrades.
- **Earnings Revisions:** Positive and broad. 
Industrials are still supported by increased capital spending in key growth areas like electricity capacity, AI infrastructure construction, defense, and energy.

### 2. WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** 
AI infrastructure beneficiaries — semiconductors, tech hardware, industrials, and utilities tied to the AI buildout — are expected to account for roughly half of total S&P 500 earnings growth in 2026 and 2027, with semiconductor companies as the primary direct beneficiaries.

--- TODAY'S UPDATE ---
**Significant material development today — update required.** The June CPI print released this morning is a meaningful surprise that partially, but not decisively, shifts the macro picture.

---

**June CPI: A Broad Beat, But Don't Pop the Champagne**


The June CPI came in lower than expected across the board — falling a seasonally adjusted **0.4%** for the month, bringing the annual headline rate down to **3.5%**, versus the Dow Jones consensus of a 0.2% drop and a 3.8% annual rate (following May's 4.2% reading).
 
Core CPI — which strips out food and energy — was **flat on the month**, putting the 12-month core rate at **2.6%**, well below the consensus of +0.2% monthly and 2.9% annually.
 This is a genuine double beat on both headline and core, and represents real progress on the disinflation front. The core number in particular, if sustained, would meaningfully shift the Fed calculus.

**The Energy Distortion & Geopolitical Caveat**


The energy index slumped **5.7%** in June — its biggest monthly drop since April 2020 — though it still surged **15.7%** on an annual basis, pushed by a 26.7% gain in gasoline.
 
An easing of hostilities helped drive oil costs roughly 25% lower in June, but President Trump last week declared the Iran ceasefire over as both sides exchanged attacks — and oil spiked Monday and was higher again Tuesday.
 This is the critical caveat: today's soft print is heavily energy-driven, and that tailwind may already be reversing. 
The concern is that this relief will be short-lived as the Iran conflict re-escalates — and it's too uncertain to know how the inflation story ends.


**Fed Reaction & Market Repricing**


Fed Chair Warsh immediately pushed back on any complacency: "There might be some that look at this morning's data and say, 'Oh, mission accomplished, everything is swell.' That is not my view."
 
Fed Governor Waller, speaking Monday, said it would take **several months of positive readings** to convince him that inflation is moving back to the 2% target.
 
Stock market futures were mostly positive following the report, while Treasury yields fell sharply. Traders continued to expect a Fed **hike in September**, though odds dropped to **63%** from better than 75% the day prior, per CME FedWatch.


**Bottom Line for the Macro Regime:** Yesterday's framework largely holds structurally, but requires one key update — core PCE will likely track meaningfully softer for June than previously assumed, reducing the urgency of a near-term hike but not eliminating it. The Fed remains hawkishly biased, the real policy rate may inch slightly more restrictive as inflation expectations adjust, and geopolitical risk in the Middle East represents the single largest upside inflation wildcard. 
As one analyst put it: "The longer the conflict drags on, the higher the probability that the Fed will have to hike and back its promise from Warsh's first meeting to 'deliver on price stability.'"

## 1. 🔴 SINGLE-STOCK BLOW-UP RISK — Top 5 Names

### ① TSLA — *Tesla, Inc.*
**Risk: Valuation fiction priced on unmonetized optionality**


Tesla is down 9.33% YTD
 and the fundamental thesis is deeply bifurcated. 
Forward EPS of $1.90 implies a forward P/E of 316x at the $600 bull-case target
 — and that *only* works on Robotaxi/Optimus delivery. 
In mid-2026, US safety regulators opened fresh investigations into Tesla's driver-assistance systems.
 
Auto sales declined in 2025 facing BYD competition, and high interest rates and regulatory hurdles for autonomous driving pose further risks
, alongside massive capital spending needs. 
A high-profile FSD safety incident could trigger regulatory backlash, pushing profitability to 2030+, and if the stock re-rates from a tech multiple back toward automotive (15–20× P/E), it implies an 80% decline from peak on automotive-only earnings.
 **Bear trigger**: one fatal Robotaxi incident, a state-level AV ban, or Q3 delivery miss.

what derails all the AI names together is an AI capex reset.

### TAIL RISK #1: AI Capex Bubble Deflation & Earnings Disappointment
**Probability: ELEVATED**


The same forces driving equities higher are concentrating risk. Global earnings growth has become increasingly dependent on a relatively small set of companies tied to AI capital spending. AI capex could continue to grow, but market leadership has narrowed significantly, with a small subset of stocks driving a disproportionate share of performance.
 
Financial markets have become more tied to the broader AI cycle. If returns on AI investment disappoint, market expectations will need to readjust if capital spending slows and financing conditions tighten. The potential impact could extend across the broader markets given the increased concentration.


**Trigger**: Any hyperscaler (MSFT, GOOGL, AMZN, META) signals capex reduction or ROI miss on AI investments. A DeepSeek-style efficiency shock proving current GPU demand is 30–40% above steady-state would be the catalyst.

**Sectors hurt most**: NVDA (–40–60%), AMD, MRVL, ANET, AVGO, ALAB, ARM, MU. Secondary: MSFT, GOOGL (multiple compression). Cloud software (SNOW, PLTR) would also re-rate violently.

**Probability: ELEVATED (Active Right Now)**


In 2026, the U.S. and Israel's war with Iran caused oil prices to rise by over 50%.
